summary: A Roman general and statesman (106-48 BC) who formed the unofficial First Triumvirate with Julius Caesar and Marcus Licinius Crassus. Clues most often identify him through that alliance, his suppression of the Cilician pirates under the Lex Gabinia, his defeat of Mithridates VI of Pontus, and his eventual civil war against Caesar, which ended in his defeat at the Battle of Pharsalus and his beheading in Egypt on the orders of Ptolemy XIII.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Clues most commonly identify Pompey through the First Triumvirate he formed with Julius Caesar and Marcus Licinius Crassus, his marriage to Caesar's daughter Julia, and his defeat and beheading in Egypt after losing the Battle of Pharsalus to Caesar. Cicero's speech in support of the Lex Gabinia, praising his military knowledge, prestige, excellence, and luck, is a recurring identifying clue.

clues.clue: Formed the First Triumvirate with Marcus Licinius Crassus and Julius Caesar
examples: name this Roman general who formed the First Triumvirate along with Crassus and Julius Caesar.
Name this military general and a member of the First Triumvirate alongside Julius Caesar and Marcus Crassus.

clues.clue: Cicero praised him for having military knowledge, prestige, excellence, and luck in a speech supporting the Lex Gabinia
examples: Cicero praised this person for having military knowledge, prestige, excellence, and luck, in a speech which helped him get appointed by the Lex Gabinia to deal with the Cilician pirates.
He was proclaimed a perfect commander because he had military knowledge, excellence, prestige, and luck in a speech by Cicero.

clues.clue: Married Julia, the daughter of his main rival Julius Caesar, as part of a political alliance; after losing to Caesar at Pharsalus, he was killed on the orders of Ptolemy XIII, who sent his head to Caesar
examples: This person was killed by Ptolemy XIII after losing the battle of Pharsalus to his main rival, whose daughter, Julia, he married.
This man's severed head was sent by Ptolemy XIII as a gift to his main rival, whose daughter Julia he married.

clues.clue: Plutarch paired his biography with that of the Spartan king Agesilaus II
examples: Plutarch paired this man's biography with that of Agesilaus II.

clues.clue: Had two sons, Gnaeus (named after him) and Sextus, who later rebelled against Octavian in Sicily
examples: This man had two sons, Sextus, who later rebelled against Octavian in Sicily, and Gnaeus, named after his father.

works.name: Early Military Career: Sulla's Civil War and the Sertorian War
works.description: Pompey rose to prominence as a young commander fighting on Sulla's side in Rome's civil war, earning a brutal nickname for his ruthlessness, then spent years in Spain helping suppress the renegade governor Sertorius, ultimately winning Rome's highest honor despite never having held elected office.

clues.clue: Earned the nickname "the Young Butcher" after executing the consul Carbo and his supporters during Sulla's civil war
examples: This man earned the sobriquet of "The Young Butcher" after executing Carbo and his supporters.

clues.clue: Was the only member of the equestrian class ever awarded a Triumph, first receiving one after his African victories (which also earned him his epithet "Magnus"); he was unable to use elephants to draw his chariot because they did not fit through the city gates
examples: He was the only equestrian to ever be awarded a triumph, receiving his first after his African victories, which also earned him his epithet, although he was unable to use elephants to draw his chariot…
Instead of using horses during one of his triumphs, this general attempted to have his chariot drawn by four elephants only to have them not fit through the gates.

clues.clue: Worked alongside Quintus Caecilius Metellus Pius in Spain to suppress the revolt of the renegade governor Sertorius, losing the battles of Lauron and Sucro (the latter after failing to wait for Metellus) before winning through a war of attrition
examples: He worked with Quintus Caecilius Metellus Pius to put down the revolt of Sertorius.
This man failed to win the battle of Sucro because he didn't wait for Quintus Caecilius Metellus Pius, who he had been sent to reinforce in the Sertorian War.

clues.clue: Drove the rebellious former consul Lepidus out of Italy
examples: This man drove the rebellious former consul Lepidus out of Italy.

works.name: Suppression of Spartacus and First Consulship
works.description: Returning from Spain, Pompey mopped up the remnants of Spartacus's slave revolt and took credit for ending it, angering Marcus Licinius Crassus, who had done most of the actual fighting. The two rivals nonetheless served together as consuls in 70 BC despite Pompey's legal ineligibility for the office.

clues.clue: Angered Crassus by taking credit for ending the revolt of Spartacus, despite Crassus having done most of the fighting
examples: This man worked to restore Cicero to Rome and had earlier angered Crassus by taking credit for ending the revolt of Spartacus.

clues.clue: Served as consul alongside Crassus in 70 BC, despite never having held a lower elected office
examples: This really, really rich man was consul with Pompey in 70 BC, and then joined with Pompey and Julius Caesar in the First Triumvirate.

works.name: Campaign Against the Cilician Pirates
works.description: In 67 BC the tribune Aulus Gabinius passed a law granting Pompey sweeping, unprecedented authority over the entire Mediterranean coastline to eliminate the Cilician pirates who had been disrupting Rome's grain supply (and had once kidnapped a young Julius Caesar). Pompey completed the campaign in a fraction of his allotted time.

clues.clue: Was given extraordinary/proconsular powers over the Mediterranean coasts by the Lex Gabinia to hunt down the Cilician pirates, who had earlier kidnapped a young Julius Caesar and disrupted Rome's grain supply
examples: He was given extraordinary powers by the Lex Gabinia to hunt down Cilician pirates.
The Lex Gabinia gave this man unprecedented power to quell piracy in the Mediterranean.

clues.clue: Completed his campaign against the pirates in only three months despite being granted a three-year term
examples: Though this legislation gave its subject a term of three years, the intended feat was accomplished in only three months.

clues.clue: Won the decisive Battle of Coracesium (Korakesion), the campaign's turning point
examples: Under this legislation, a commander won the Battle of Korakesion.
The jealous consul Gaius Calpurnius Piso tried to bribe soldiers to stop this campaign, which included a decisive battle at Coracesium.

clues.clue: Divided the Mediterranean coasts into thirteen districts under subordinate legates, but personally overrode Metellus's authority to accept the surrender of pirates on Crete
examples: Its leader assigned legates with authority over thirteen designated regions, but overrode the authority of Metellus to accept the surrender of enemies on Crete.

works.name: War Against Mithridates and the Eastern Settlement
works.description: The Lex Manilia transferred command of the war against Mithridates VI of Pontus from the ineffectual Lucius Licinius Lucullus to Pompey, who finished off Mithridates and reorganized much of the eastern Mediterranean, annexing Syria and intervening in Judaea while cultivating an image as a successor to Alexander the Great.

clues.clue: Was given command against Mithridates VI of Pontus and Tigranes the Great of Armenia by the Lex Manilia, replacing the ineffective Lucius Licinius Lucullus, who resented losing the command
examples: Lucullus lost the command against Mithridates to this man via the Manilian Law.
Lucullus objected to losing his Asiatic command to this man.

clues.clue: Ultimately defeated Mithridates VI in the last of his three wars against Rome; Mithridates's own son Pharnaces II later revolted against him
examples: Pompey ultimately defeated this king of Pontus and father-in-law of Tigranes in the last of three wars named after him. This man's son, Pharnaces II, later revolted against him.

clues.clue: Cultivated an anastole hairstyle in imitation of Alexander the Great to bolster his image as Alexander's successor
examples: This man's sculptures portray him with an anastole hairstyle in an attempt to solidify his legacy as Alexander the Great's successor.

clues.clue: Annexed Syria as a Roman province and incorporated Judaea into Rome's sphere of influence
examples: This enforcer of the lex Gabinia annexed Syria and incorporated Judaea.

works.name: First Triumvirate and Political Life in Rome
works.description: Pompey's political life in Rome centered on his alliance with Caesar and Crassus, cemented by his marriage to Caesar's daughter Julia, and on his uneasy relationship with the demagogue Publius Clodius Pulcher. After Crassus's death removed the buffer between the two remaining triumvirs, Pompey drifted toward the anti-Caesarian Optimates faction.

clues.clue: Became governor of Hispania Ulterior (while remaining in Rome with his new wife Julia) as part of the political alliance that also let him settle his veterans -- the arrangement now known as the First Triumvirate
examples: This man stayed in Rome with his wife Julia after becoming the governor of Hispania Ulterior via a political alliance that also allowed him settle his veterans from campaigns such as his war against t…

clues.clue: Grieved so deeply over Julia's death that he was further pained by her burial in the Campus Martius rather than his favorite Alban estate
examples: This man's grief over his wife's death was exacerbated when was buried not in his favorite Alban estate, but instead in the Campus Martius.

clues.clue: Controlled Rome's grain (corn) supply for five years and won a hotly contested consulship opposed by Ahenobarbus, who objected to his awarding the proconsulship of Gaul to an ally
examples: This man controlled Rome's corn supply for five years and won a consulship contested by Ahenobarbus, who objected to this man's awarding of the proconsulship of Gaul to an ally.

clues.clue: Was appointed sole consul to quell the riots caused by Clodius's street gangs in the wake of Clodius's death in 52 BC
examples: Pompey was appointed sole consul to quell the riots caused by the (Publius Clodius Pulcher's) death.
This politician's supporters caused riots that Pompey was appointed sole consul to quell.

clues.clue: Arranged the consulship of Afranius, whose only real skill turned out to be dancing
examples: This man arranged the consulship of Afranius, who proved to be a failure because his only skill was dancing.

clues.clue: Drifted toward the anti-Caesarian Optimates faction after the death of Crassus at Carrhae removed the buffer holding the First Triumvirate together
examples: Pompey drifted toward the rival Optimates after the death of Crassus.
Crassus's own death at the Battle of Carrhae unraveled the Triumvirate and led to civil war between Julius Caesar and this other member.

works.name: Theater of Pompey
works.description: Built on the Campus Martius, this was Rome's first permanent theater. To skirt a censorial ban on permanent theatrical venues, its uppermost tier was disguised as steps leading to a temple of Venus Victrix. Its attached curia later became infamous as the site of Julius Caesar's assassination.

clues.clue: Was Rome's first permanent theater, built on the Campus Martius, with a temple to Venus Victrix on its top level so that it could technically be classified as a religious "sacred area" and skirt a censorial ban on permanent theaters
examples: One structure built by this man on the Campus Martius had a temple to Venus Victrix and was Rome's first permanent theatre.
Because the censors prohibited the permanent creation of structures of its kind, this building's top level contained a temple to Venus Victrix.

clues.clue: Its 55 BC opening ceremonies featured the actor Clodius Aesopus and were marred by an ill-omened elephant fight
examples: Plutarch writes that this building's opening was marred by the "terrifying spectacle" of an elephant fight, in which the dying animals' trumpeting marked an ill omen.
Clodius Aesopus was brought out of retirement for the 55 B.C. opening ceremonies of this building.

clues.clue: Its attached curia (assembly hall) served as a temporary Senate meeting place in the 40s BC and was the site of Julius Caesar's assassination in 44 BC
examples: It was often used as a temporary Senate meeting place in the 40s BC, so in an ironic twist, its curia was where Julius Caesar was murdered.
A senate meeting in the Theater of Pompey was the site of the 44 BCE assassination of this man.

works.name: Civil War with Caesar and Death in Egypt
works.description: The collapse of the First Triumvirate led to civil war between Pompey and Caesar. Pompey won an indecisive early victory at Dyrrhachium but was routed at Pharsalus, fled to Egypt, and was assassinated on the orders of the boy-king Ptolemy XIII in a bid to win Caesar's favor.

clues.clue: Won the Battle of Dyrrhachium, prompting Caesar to withdraw his forces, before being decisively defeated at the Battle of Pharsalus shortly after
examples: This victor at Dyrrachium was assigned Hispania at the Lucca conference.
The Battle of Pharsalus was preceded by this other conflict. Despite being indecisive, Pompey is generally credited as the victor because Caesar withdrew his forces.

clues.clue: Debated the concept of providence with the philosopher Cratippus of Pergamon after his defeat, according to Plutarch
examples: According to Plutarch, this man had a brief debate on providence with Cratippus of Pergamon after losing one battle.

clues.clue: Fled to Egypt after Pharsalus, where the eunuch Pothinus convinced the boy-king Ptolemy XIII to have him killed as he landed at Alexandria in 48 BC
examples: Pothinus convinced Ptolemy XIII to betray this man in Egypt and send his head to a rival who had beaten him at the battle of Pharsalus.
It was taken by three conspirators as its original owner landed at Alexandria in 48 BCE.

clues.clue: His body was cremated on a pyre by his freedman Philip
examples: This man's body was burnt on a pyre by his freedman Philip.

clues.clue: His severed head, presented to Caesar along with his signet ring, reportedly enraged rather than pleased Caesar, who ordered Pothinus and Achillas executed
examples: Achillas and Pothinus were ordered executed by the man who was enraged by receiving this object along with a signet ring.
name this object, which was cut off of the body of a noted scourge of pirates in an attempt to curry favor with Julius Caesar.

clues.clue: His head was later used in the founding of a temple to Nemesis, in view of his widow Cornelia
examples: This object was used in the foundation of a temple to Nemesis after it was acquired in view of a certain Cornelia.

comprehensive_summary: Pompey the Great (106-48 BC) was a Roman general and statesman whose career began under Sulla's patronage during Rome's civil war, where he earned the grim nickname "the Young Butcher" for executing the consul Carbo and his supporters. He became the only Roman of equestrian rank ever to be awarded a Triumph (earning both the honor and his epithet "Magnus" after African victories, though his attempt to have the chariot drawn by elephants failed when they could not fit through the gates), and he spent years in Spain alongside Quintus Caecilius Metellus Pius suppressing the renegade governor Sertorius. Returning to Italy, he mopped up the remnants of Spartacus's slave revolt and controversially took credit for ending it, angering Marcus Licinius Crassus, with whom he nonetheless served as consul in 70 BC. In 67 BC the Lex Gabinia granted Pompey unprecedented authority over the entire Mediterranean to eliminate the Cilician pirates, a campaign he completed in just three months, winning the decisive Battle of Coracesium. The following year the Lex Manilia gave him command against Mithridates VI of Pontus and Tigranes the Great of Armenia, replacing the ineffective Lucius Licinius Lucullus; Pompey ultimately defeated Mithridates, annexed Syria as a Roman province, and intervened in Judaea, all while styling his hair in an anastole to evoke Alexander the Great. In 59 BC he entered an informal political alliance with Julius Caesar and Crassus -- the First Triumvirate -- cemented by his marriage to Caesar's daughter Julia; her death and burial in the Campus Martius devastated him. He also built Rome's first permanent theater, the Theater of Pompey, disguising its top tier as a temple to Venus Victrix to evade a censorial ban on permanent theatrical venues; its attached curia would later become the site of Caesar's assassination in 44 BC. Pompey's political career in Rome included controlling the city's grain supply, feuding with the demagogue Publius Clodius Pulcher (whose death in 52 BC led to riots that Pompey was appointed sole consul to quell), and, after Crassus's death at Carrhae removed the buffer between the two remaining triumvirs, drifting toward the anti-Caesarian Optimates faction. Civil war with Caesar followed: Pompey won an indecisive victory at Dyrrhachium but was routed at the Battle of Pharsalus in 48 BC, then fled to Egypt, where the eunuch Pothinus convinced the boy-king Ptolemy XIII to have him assassinated upon landing at Alexandria in a bid to win Caesar's favor. His body was burned by his freedman Philip, and his severed head, presented to Caesar, reportedly provoked grief and outrage rather than gratitude, leading Caesar to have Pothinus and Achillas executed.
recursive_suggestions: Publius Clodius Pulcher -- surfaced as its own answerline with dense, independent content (Bona Dea scandal, feud with Cicero, street gangs, death and its riots); only the Pompey-relevant riot-suppression fact was pulled into this analysis.
Mithridates VI -- recurring bonus answer (2x) with independent content on his three wars against Rome; could support a standalone page distinct from Pompey's campaign against him.
Battle of Pharsalus -- recurring bonus answer (2x) with independent battle-narrative content; currently folded into this figure's civil-war section.
Theater of Pompey -- has enough independently-answerlined content (construction, opening ceremonies, Caesar's assassination) to eventually support its own architecture/landmark page.

Ambiguity flag: "Lucius Cornelius Sulla Felix AND Marcus Licinius Crassus" surfaced as a joint answerline describing interactions between those two men specifically (not Pompey) and was excluded as a different-topic answerline, despite being picked up by the "Pompey" search.

Ambiguity flag: several bonus parts about Massalia (Marseille), the Vascones/Basques, Illyria and Queen Teuta, the Aetolian League, and general Roman Triumph customs surfaced from background details referencing Pompey but have different actual answerlines; excluded except where a fact was specifically and clearly about Pompey himself.
